diagrams.title: Hierarchical Directory Tree
diagrams.description: A tree diagram showing the hierarchical organization of directories starting from the root directory, branching into subdirectories like /home, /etc, /var, and /usr, with files and further nested directories. This illustrates the parent-child relationship and the tree-like structure of file systems.
elements: Level 3: files like documents.txt, config.conf, program.exe
Directory folder icons and file document icons
diagrams.purpose: Explains how directories are organized in a hierarchical tree structure, demonstrating the root directory, subdirectories, and files with their parent-child relationships.

diagrams.title: Absolute vs Relative Path Navigation
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a directory tree with two highlighted path examples: an absolute path starting from root (/home/user/documents/report.txt) and a relative path from a current working directory (../images/photo.jpg). Arrows demonstrate navigation direction.
elements: Legend distinguishing absolute vs relative paths
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the difference between absolute paths (complete path from root) and relative paths (path relative to current directory), helping understand path navigation in operating systems.

diagrams.title: Directory Entry Structure
diagrams.description: A detailed view of what a directory contains internally, showing directory entries with fields like filename, inode number, entry type (file/directory), and permissions. Shows how directories act as tables mapping names to inodes.
elements: Entry rows showing: filename, inode number, type, permissions
Inode block pointing to actual file data
Arrows linking directory entries to their inodes
File data blocks at the bottom
diagrams.purpose: Explains the internal structure of directories - how they store mappings between filenames and inode numbers, enabling the OS to locate files and navigate the file system.
